perturbation.original_text: If the Investment Adviser is required to reimburse the Fund, the Investment Adviser shall make such payment within 30 days after each month-end in the amount due the Fund as of each month.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Investment Adviser is required to reimburse the Fund, the Investment Adviser shall make such payment within 90 days after each month-end in the amount due the Fund as of each month.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the reimbursement timeframe to 90 days may violate regulations requiring prompt payment, especially if the fund operates under ERISA guidelines. The promptness of payments may affect plan assets and participant benefits. This extended timeframe could potentially create a conflict of interest if the delay benefits the investment adviser at the expense of the fund's beneficiaries.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 29 CFR § 2550.404a-1 - Fiduciary duties under section 404(a)(1) of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974, as amended
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation outlines the fiduciary duties under ERISA, which include acting prudently and solely in the interest of plan participants and beneficiaries. Delaying reimbursement payments for 90 days may violate the prudence requirement if it harms the fund's performance or creates unnecessary risk, and could violate the exclusive purpose rule if the delay benefits the adviser more than the fund.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 29 CFR § 2550.404a-1 - Investment duties. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 29 CFR § 2550.404a-1 - Investment duties. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 2550.404a-1 Investment duties. (a) In general. Sections 404(a)(1)(A) and 404(a)(1)(B) of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 , as amended ( ERISA or the Act) provide, in part, that a fiduciary shall discharge that person's duties with respect to the plan solely in the interests of the participants and beneficiaries; for the exclusive purpose of providing benefits to participants and their beneficiaries and defraying reasonable expenses of administering the plan; and with the care, skill, prudence , and diligence under the circumstances then prevailing that a prudent person acting in a like capacity and familiar with such matters would use in the conduct of an enterprise of a like character and with like aims. (b) Investment prudence duties. (1) With regard to the consideration of an investment or investment course of action taken by a fiduciary of an employee benefit plan pursuant to the fiduciary's investment duties, the requirements of section 404(a)(1)(B) of the Act set forth in paragraph (a) of this section are satisfied if the fiduciary: (i) Has given appropriate consideration to those facts and circumstances that, given the scope of such fiduciary's investment duties, the fiduciary knows or should know are relevant to the particular investment or investment course of action involved, including the role the investment or investment course of action plays in that portion of the plan's investment portfolio or menu with respect to which the fiduciary has investment duties; and (ii) Has acted accordingly. (2) For purposes of paragraph (b)(1) of this section, “appropriate consideration” shall include, but is not necessarily limited to: (i) A determination by the fiduciary that the particula
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the reimbursement timeframe from 30 to 90 days, which contradicts ERISA's fiduciary duties. Snippet 2, referencing 29 CFR § 2550.404a-1, states fiduciaries must act "solely in the interests of the participants and beneficiaries" and with "care, skill, prudence, and diligence." Delaying reimbursement for 90 days could harm the fund's performance, conflicting with acting prudently and solely in the beneficiaries' interest, thus violating ERISA regulations.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall continue in effect for a period of one year from the date of its execution and from year to year thereafter provided such continuance is specifically approved by a majority of the trustees of the Trust who (i) are not "interested persons" of the Trust or any other party to this Agreement, as defined in the 1940 Act, and (ii) have no direct or indirect financial interest in the operation of this Agreement ("Non- Interested Trustees").
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall continue in effect for a period of five years from the date of its execution and shall automatically renew unless a simple majority of all Trustees votes to terminate it.
perturbation.explanation: The Investment Company Act of 1940 requires annual approval of agreements like this by disinterested trustees. Extending the term to five years with automatic renewal undermines this requirement. This removes the safeguard for fund shareholders.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 15(c) of the Investment Company Act of 1940
perturbation.law_citation: Section 15(c) of the Investment Company Act of 1940
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(c) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires the board of directors, including a majority of the independent directors, to approve the advisory agreement annually. The modified text, which allows for a five-year term with automatic renewal unless terminated, directly contradicts the annual review and approval requirement mandated by the Act.

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the agreement's term to five years with automatic renewal, which contradicts the Investment Company Act of 1940's requirement for annual approval by disinterested trustees, as explained in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not Found' error, and Snippet 2 is 'None', the consistent explanations provided regarding Section 15(c) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 clearly establish that the automatic renewal clause removes the safeguard provided to fund shareholders by the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will terminate upon the termination of the Advisory Agreement except the Investment Adviser's Right of Recovery set forth in section 1.5 herein.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will continue indefinitely, regardless of the termination of the Advisory Agreement, and the Investment Adviser's Right of Recovery as set forth in section 1.5 herein will remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.explanation: If the advisory agreement is terminated, then maintaining the net investment income maintenance agreement in perpetuity with a right of recovery may violate the Investment Company Act of 1940, and fiduciary duties, as it means the Fund may be continuously obligated to pay an entity that is no longer providing advisory services. This indefinite continuation and recovery right is potentially abusive and would violate regulations requiring fair dealing and prudent management.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 36(b) of the Investment Company Act of 1940
perturbation.law_citation: Section 36(b) of the Investment Company Act of 1940
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 36(b) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 imposes a fiduciary duty on investment advisers with respect to compensation received from the fund. By continuing the agreement indefinitely after the advisory agreement's termination and preserving the adviser's right of recovery, there is a risk of the adviser receiving excessive compensation for no current service, potentially violating this fiduciary duty.

perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by requiring the agreement to continue indefinitely, regardless of the advisory agreement's termination, and preserving the adviser's right of recovery. This contradicts fiduciary duties as the Investment Company Act of 1940 imposes a fiduciary duty on investment advisers with respect to compensation received from the fund. The indefinite continuation after termination could mean excessive compensation for no service.
